exemplar.title: To use Voronoi diagrams to assess the distribution of emergency services within the city of Florence, Italy, and determine whether new emergency service locations are needed for optimal coverage.

detailedExemplarData.title: To use Voronoi diagrams to assess the distribution of emergency services within the city of Florence, Italy, and determine whether new emergency service locations are needed for optimal coverage.

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Effective use of headings, tables and figures to guide the reader
Highly relevant content focused on Voronoi diagrams and emergency service placement
weaknesses: Minor interruptions in flow due to midâtext appendices and repeated aim statements
Inconsistent reference formatting and occasional typographical errors
Some sections restate definitions or results, reducing conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent with clear logical development and wellâorganized overall.  The structure (introduction/body/conclusion) is easy to follow, and most material is directly relevant.  However, interruptions from appendix tables, repeated definitions, inconsistent bibliography formatting and minor typos prevent a band-4 award.  A band-3 score (3/4) accurately reflects the quality demonstrated.

strengths: Correct use of standard notation for gradients, midpoints and vector coordinates
Multiple complementary representations (maps, plots, tables) support the analysis
Clear terminology section defining Voronoi elements
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (e.g., mixture of determinant and arrow forms in the shoelace formula)
Blurred figures and unconnected captions (particularly Figures 7â8) hinder clarity
Inconsistent rounding and significantâfigure usage reduce precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student employs appropriate mathematical language and uses several representations, repeated formatting inconsistencies and unclear connections between figures significantly impede communication.  These recurring issues warrant a band-2 score (2/4) rather than the band-3 midpoint.

strengths: Evidence of independent thinking in selecting Florence and extending the model with two new sites
A clear personal narrative linking childhood experiences to the exploration context
Significant creativity shown by integrating population considerations with Voronoi extensions
weaknesses: Exploration still follows standard textbook procedures for much of the mathematics
Personal approach drives parts of the work but is less evident in later technical sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows significant independent thinking, a clear personal approach and notable creativity in model enhancements.  However, the reliance on familiar procedures prevents an outstanding personal engagement rating, placing the work solidly in band-2 (2/3).

strengths: Meaningful reflection on model limitations (service capacity, traffic) and proposed improvements
Critical acknowledgment of assumptions and their impact on results
Clear linkage between Voronoi/shoelace findings and emergencyâresponse understanding
weaknesses: Reflection is concentrated in the conclusion and not embedded throughout the exploration
Lacks quantitative support for some improvement suggestions (e.g., recomputed areas for new sites)
Discussion of data sources and integration methods is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful reflection with some critical analysis and makes clear connections to understanding.  However, the treatment is not sustained across the work and lacks depth in places, placing it in band-2 (2/3).

strengths: Use of gradients, midpoints, perpendicular bisectors and the shoelace algorithm at SL level
Accurate area calculations and coherent linkage of geometric construction to real-world context
Stepwise shoelace example demonstrates solid understanding
weaknesses: Border choice (MâNâOâP) introduced without geometric justification, affecting area validity
Rounding inconsistencies and one inverted reciprocal slope error affect precision
Populationâdensity integration is mentioned but not quantified or modelled mathematically
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration applies relevant course-level mathematics effectively and with generally high accuracy, meriting a band-4 standard.  However, significant methodological gaps (border justification, rounding errors, omitted data integration) prevent full rigor.  A band-3 score (3/6) reflects the solid but imperfect use of mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting Florence, collecting real hospital data, and deciding to extend the model with two new facilities demonstrate significant independent thinking, but the mathematical procedures themselves largely follow textbook methods, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The student demonstrates good understanding of how Voronoi cell boundaries arise from perpendicular bisectors and how cell area informs service coverage.  Explanations are generally sound though not rigorously theoretical, indicating good (not yet thorough and rigorous) conceptual grasp.

Calculations are largely correct; spot-checking midpoint and gradient values confirms accuracy and final area values are internally consistent.  Minor rounding inconsistencies (e.g. negative reciprocal mishandled once) prevent a higher band, but overall accuracy is high with only minor errors.

Most material directly supports the aim; however several explanatory paragraphs repeat definitions of vertices and edges or restate results already given, so the exploration is ârelevant and mostly conciseâ rather than âhighly conciseâ.

Standard notation for gradients, midpoints and line equations is used correctly, and vectors such as (xâ,yâ) are identified.  Some slips occur (confusing  â1/m with 1/m in the table), and the modulus symbols in the shoelace formula are formatted inconsistently, so language is âmostly appropriateâ rather than fully consistent.

Explanations generally match the notation and diagrams, allowing the reader to follow the argument.  Occasional typographical errors and inconsistent rounding (e.g. gradient rounded then reused) reduce overall consistency, so the strand sits firmly in the âclear and mostly consistentâ band.

Gradient, midpoint, perpendicular bisector equations, Voronoi construction and the shoelace algorithm are all at or above the SL syllabus level and are applied effectively to a real-world context.  The exploration extends the basic model by adding new sites and recomputing areas, demonstrating effective use of relevant mathematics.

The student employs multiple representations: annotated maps, Cartesian plots, Voronoi diagrams, tables of coordinates and algebraic equations.  These representations complement each other well, yet some figures are blurred and the relationship between Figure 7 and Figure 8 is not fully explained, preventing a âsophisticatedâ rating.

The student reflects on model limitations (equal service capacity, traffic conditions) and proposes improvements, indicating meaningful reflection.  The discussion, however, is brief and not embedded throughout the work, so it falls short of âsubstantial critical reflectionâ.

Using Voronoi diagrams in conjunction with a population-density discussion and proposing extra sites shows creativity and initiative beyond a purely procedural exercise, yet the solutions remain relatively straightforward; thus âsignificantâ creativity is demonstrated.

The report follows a clear progression (introduction â methodology â results â conclusion) and the mathematical arguments build on one another logically.  Minor jumps occur when describing how borders M-N-O-P were chosen and when moving from area results to recommendations, so the development is not completely seamless, but overall coherence is evident.

Some critical analysis is presentâcomparing rural and urban cell sizes and linking these to response timesâbut the analysis relies mainly on area size without deeper statistical validation; thus the strand aligns with âmeaningful analysis with some critiqueâ.

The student successfully links Voronoi and Shoelace results to emergency-response understanding, showing a clear connection to mathematical insight.  The enhancement of understanding is moderate rather than strong, so band 2 is appropriate.

Headings, sub-headings, tables and figures are used effectively and an introduction/body/conclusion structure can be followed without difficulty.  Nevertheless some sections (e.g. extensive appendix tables placed mid-text) interrupt the flow, preventing a truly seamless organisation.

The studentâs narrative is clearly personalâreferencing childhood experience and designing bespoke city limitsâbut the personal approach drives only part of the exploration; much of the work follows standard algorithmic steps.  Hence a âclear personal approachâ is visible, meriting band 2.

annotations.comment: The title concisely conveys the purpose and scope, providing clear focus on assessing emergency service locations with Voronoi diagrams.
Consider including the studentâs name, date, and context on the title page to enhance clarity and meet formal academic presentation standards.
The introduction provides a solid definition of Voronoi diagrams, but the first paragraph is overly long and repeats phrases, which affects conciseness.
The personal narrative about childhood experiences in Florence effectively demonstrates genuine engagement and helps contextualize the exploration.
Methodology clearly states the use of gradient and midpoint formulas, but does not detail how population density data will be quantified or integrated into the model.
The methodological description of mapping coordinates onto a Cartesian graph is logically structured and demonstrates clear understanding of the modelling steps.
Figure 3 and Figure 4 are presented, but the captions do not explain how they connect; the reader needs clearer guidance on the sequence of representations.
The combination of map overlays, Cartesian plots, and tabulated coordinates demonstrates effective use of multiple representations to support analysis.
The coordinate table uses inconsistent significant figures (e.g., 7.80, -4.70, 0.500), which can affect the precision of subsequent calculations.
The terminology section provides clear, consistent definitions of key Voronoi elements, strengthening reader understanding of cells, edges, and vertices.
The gradient equation is correctly presented, but the explanation should specify the ordering of points (xâ,yâ vs. xâ,yâ) to avoid ambiguity in application.
The sign of the reciprocal is inverted incorrectly: 1/(-2.027) remains -0.493, not +0.493. This affects all subsequent bisector equations.
Including detailed algebraic steps when rearranging to y=mx+c would enhance the clarity and reproducibility of the perpendicular-bisector derivations.
The choice of borders MâNâOâP to close open cells is introduced without geometric justification; this decision directly impacts area calculations and requires rationale.
The primary Voronoi diagram clearly displays coverage cells against city boundaries, effectively linking geometric construction to realâworld context.
Typographical error in âVoroniu diagramâ could confuse readers; ensure all key terms are spelled accurately to maintain professionalism.
The aim is restated before the shoelace section; consider condensing repeated aims to improve conciseness and focus on methodology.
The shoelace formula is presented using both determinant and arrow notation, which can be confusing; standardise to one notation for clarity.
Explicitly include absoluteâvalue bars around the shoelace summation in formulas to emphasise the need for a nonnegative area result.
The vertex coordinates for cell A are misformatted (e.g., âH (23.2, 10.0)1.98)â); ensure each coordinate pair is clearly separated and correctly recorded.
The example shoelace calculation for cell A demonstrates a thorough stepwise process, reinforcing the studentâs comprehension of polygon area computation.
The rationale for choosing new site locations is described qualitatively but lacks quantitative support from recomputed Voronoi areas to validate the proposal.
Include recalculated cell areas and updated diagrams after adding new sites to quantitatively demonstrate the improvement in coverage.
In the table for bisector calculations, rounding of intercepts and slopes is inconsistent; maintain 3 significant figures throughout to uphold precision.
Indicating an estimated error margin when rounding slopes would strengthen the accuracy discussion and show awareness of computational sensitivity.
Figure 8 is blurred and the newly allocated vertices are difficult to read, which reduces the precision of subsequent Shoelace calculations.
Extending the Voronoi diagram to incorporate two new sites shows notable creativity and personal initiative in exploring model enhancements.
The conclusion restates findings effectively but does not integrate limitations or model assumptions throughout the body to reinforce critical analysis.
The evaluation section acknowledges key model assumptions (resource equality, traffic conditions), demonstrating meaningful reflection on limitations.
The improvement suggestions are insightful but would benefit from specifying data sources and analytical methods (e.g., traffic datasets, GIS layers).
Recognizing the impact of traffic conditions and alternative transport modes adds valuable critical perspective that could inform future model refinements.
References are formatted inconsistently and some entries lack complete access dates or authors, reducing the professionalism of the bibliography.
Adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) and ensure each source includes author, title, date, and access information for clarity and reliability.
